### antoramo — 2016-09-10T02:32:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol-issues.18402/post-201214>

Hi everyone.
I am 37 male who loves sports. Gym and running.

Right now I am facing a peculiar medical situation and I am trying to get some valid input. Doctors couldn´t help that much.

-   I've extremely high urine free cortisol (larger than 500 when the max is 60), I repeated ufc24 7 times on 4 different laboratories with no significant differences.
-   On salivary cortisol i have circadin rythm. Cushing disease has been rullet out.
-   Blood sometimes is higher than max, others near max. Ive got a midnight serum cortisol of 14 (max 8).

My symptoms are:

-   muscle wasting
-   increse body fat on chest and belly ( Ive lost a lot of weight but i am having more and more fat on my body)
-   insominas (rarely)
-   I've got mild osteopenia.
-   No hypertension, No diabetes, no cushing stigmata.

Doctors put me in surveillance, ordered me to stop any supplements and i'll redo labs at beggining Octuber.

What situations, besides cushings, can give you these obscene cortisol values? Is this somekind of adrenal disfunction? what can i do about it ?

Many thks,
Best,
Tó

### caroline — 2016-09-10T07:46:32+0300

Are you still doing all the crazy sports?

what is your NNemf exposure? computers etc.?????

do you wear blue blockers?

Do you listen to Jack's podcasts? easy fast way for info

Do you have Jack's book? it is the first couple of years of the blog.

What do you eat? drink?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-11T01:57:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol-issues.18402/post-201266>

Complete light mismatch does this. You dont fix the biochemical substrtate (cortisol molecule) problem you fix the things that cause it........Time 22 lays that idea out. ### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-10T17:03:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/superoxide-generation-how-it-occurs-it-is-absent-in-t2d.18404/post-201249>

Mitochondria consume more than 90% of the molecular oxygen available and are considered to the major sites of ROS production in aerobic cells (Boveris and Chance 1973). Approximately, 2% of the molecular oxygen consumed during respiration is converted into the superoxide anion radical, the precursor of most ROS. Normally, a four-electron reduction of O2, resulting in the production of two molecules of water, is catalyzed by complex IV of the mitochondrial respiratory chain. However, the electron transport chain contains several redox centers (e.g., in complexes I and III) that can leak electrons to molecular oxygen, serving as the primary source of superoxide production. The one electron reduction of oxygen is thermodynamically favorable for most mitochondrial oxidoreductases. ROS, if not detoxifed, oxidize cellular proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids and, by doing so, may cause cell dysfunction or death. Oxidative stress, that is, an imbalance that favors the production of ROS over antioxidant defenses, has been implicated in a wide variety of pathologies, including malignant and neurodegenerative diseases. To prevent this, mitochondria contain a host of water and lipid soluble antioxidants and antioxidant enzymes. How much do you know about these critical mechanisms in mitochondria? [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3977679/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3977679/)

# Thread: How to get UVB with out burning
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-get-uvb-with-out-burning.18408/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lawrencep93 — 2016-09-11T04:33:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-get-uvb-with-out-burning.18408/post-201272>

So I am trying to get plenty of sun, close to naked in my backyard, barefoot.

I try to get sun in the AM of rising, then generally go back inside and do my usual morning stuff and or get ready for work depending on the day.

Then if I am home I try to get outside close to naked around 10-11am (UVB in Melbourne Australia in spring now seems to start around 9:30am to 3:30pm from Dminder 3-4UVI).
Or when I finish work (not grounded, usually t-shirt and pants, but change into shorts, walking outside from work.

I tend to avoid mid day sun here in Australia because of the donut hole.

The issue I have found is that I seem to be burning, not 100% sure if it is me burning or my blood coming to the surface to absorb UV light.

The last time I tested this my skin was still pink/red for 2 days.

So am I absorbing UV light and then am a little slow to assimilate it so my skin stays pink for longer or am I getting mild sun burn.

I am currently super white and want to build up a little colour in my skin heading into summer/ better sun tolerance for the middle of summer.

My fish intake was a little on the lower side over winter, but now coming into spring I am increasing my intake, 1 dozen oysters every friday fresh from my market, also got prawns this week. Adding in White fish from NZ that is fresh and abundant at my fish supplier for cheap, with my goal to keep my fish intake to 2-3 meals per week minimum, I think I had fish on average once per week during winter. Also they have raw tuna sushimi back in stock at my fish supplier so I think I will be getting back on that, only issue is that it is $50/kg.

Following the Leptin RX, ketogenic diet similar to the Epi-paleo, but I still use some nightshades like chilli.
Trying to spend more time outside but I find sunburn is an issue for me.
Inside homr I have red lights, and use blue blockers at work (pavo 50% coverage SFW glasses) and full blue blockers the ugly ones at home.

My Omega-6 should not be too high, however my chicken intake may be a concern in regards to if my omega-6 fatty acids have increased over winter, been eating a lot of organic chicken hearts, cheap source of protein and coQ10.

If you need any other info ask me

### Lahelada — 2016-09-11T16:54:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-get-uvb-with-out-burning.18408/post-201297>

Things that jump out at me:

\- up your DHA intake before spring really hits (inability to tan speaks to this)
\- try an Epsom salt spritz before going out in the sun . It helped me to tan . I kept a light tan all over winter but still cannot tan my lower legs, my thighs do accept albeit a little colour.
\- unless you are nightshade intolerant include tomato and watermelon in summer as natural "sun screen"
Even if you think you are nightshade intolerant it might be a good biohack to see if you still are . Depends on your inner doctor if you think this is wise for you at this point.
\- Omega 6 distribution : Some parts of you tan more easily ,some less,some not depending on omega 6 distribution. Watch and protect the parts that do not tan easily but do not cover them completely.

\- When you go for an extended sun session have a session between 3-5 pm as well . Also bear in mind that this window in our climes moves more than in other latitudes as summer approaches. According to d minder I am getting UVB still at 3.10 pm . So shift that window back to when UVB is not present anymore but the light has shifted to red.

\- When you read the last point above you may find that just having red light permanently at home may not be the best solution but that pairing it with black light may be a better way to go.

Just the things I did . Hope they help you.Never forget N: 1 .

### Lahelada — 2016-09-11T17:34:26+0300

### lawrencep93 — 2016-09-12T00:45:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-get-uvb-with-out-burning.18408/post-201323>

Interestingly my first session of sun lead me to being red for 2-3 days.

Yesterday did a slightly shorter session, but with in 12 hours the redness that my family was yelling at me about is completely gone.

I think I may have found my sweet spot, also my back seems better than my chest? Is that due to more omega-3 being around BAT?

Jack I wish I had trees at my house I sat naked (underwear) on concrete trying to stay as connected to the ground as possible.

When I am down at my beach house I will try again but with my feet in the ocean (summer).

The part of red lights in my house is I try to sit outside as much as possible to do my work, then once the sun sets I keep my room dark, but with only some red LED lighting to see and read before bed, then I usually put a podcast on to listen in complete darkness for 1-2 hours, then turn the power off to everything and sleep.

# Thread: Optimal use of the device for IR
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-use-of-the-device-for-ir.18415/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1

### smith5 — 2016-09-11T20:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-use-of-the-device-for-ir.18415/post-201314>

Hi,
I've been following dr. Kruse's recommendations for some time in drinking more cooled spring water, eating more fish and trying to get at least 10-20 min or more sunlight a day and got quite not bad results although some better results would be possible for myself at least by other means. Now i would like to tune a device i have for optimal usage. It's a device generating vibroacoustic and infrared frequency patterns for a variety of disorders, as mentioned in its operation manual. It's manufactured in Russia and named "Vitafon-2". (Probably better than nothing). As i would like to tune for optimal use only it's IR emitter, so would briefly describe only the framework it is used in as it's described in it's operation user manual.

"Therapeutic effect of infrared radiation is linked to microcirculation enhancement in tissues, activation of physical and biochemical processes in an organism and reduction of inflammation intensity.
The power of IR-radiation changes automatically and continuously according to a preset programmed cycle from a minimum of 3 mW to a maximum of 40 mW. This produces temperature microoscillations in the area of exposure. Also, the working surface of the IR emitter is a metallic disk and its contact with the body causes a short-term reduction in temperature down 2-3 °C on the surface of the exposure area. This enhances muscle microtremor and therefore lymph efflux and venous drainage are improved.
IR radiation of the emitter has an average wavelength of 0.95 micrometers. The effect of such radiation has an anti-inflammatory nature."

So IR radiation (in conjunction with vibrophone generated preset acoustic frequency patterns) generated by this device is used for combined treatment of prostatitis, cystitis, trophic ulcers, pyelonephritis (including remission stage), arthritis, maxillary sinusitis, chronic hepatitis, lower limb lymphostasis and diabetic angiopathy. Vibroacoustic patterns alone are also used for other disorders, but i would not mention them as am going to use only IR part. Approximate IR duration for these treatments is 10 (3-15) min a day for 14-21 days (i can provide more details if necessary). IR radiation is preprogrammed by the device to be emitted in 7 different modes (not in a steady state mode) with IR power varying at various time cycles. Detailed IR power timing is depicted in the graphs attached, there's no explanation in the manual for this specific timing and i didn't search for relevant studies. The diameter of the IR emitter disk is ~5cm, and there are 3 IR radiation points at it's perimeter line at 120 degree angles (they are activated all at once).

I would like to use it in the framework of regeneration and general function improvement and I heard there may be some precautions and contraindications for IR usage.

Another optimization for IR is making more EZ water with this emitter (just radiating the IR onto the water) and drink it or use freezed. And there are several questions in relation to that:
1) what (spring) water volume should be used (i have 500, 720 ml and 3 l glass volumes for now)
2) IR exposure time (i would suppose 15-30 minutes)
3) should i drink the irradiated water immediately or can store it cooled in a dark fridge or make ice
4) can i test the irradiated water by smartphone magnetic field sensors (or maybe there are other means to see the features changed by IR).

Any ideas, suggestions?

Thanks.

Hi Videha

My computer screen has its own amber glasses .... Its a stage lighting gel, two names I know of are Rosco [https://www.rosco.com/filters/roscolux.cfm#colors](https://www.rosco.com/filters/roscolux.cfm#colors) and Lee Filters. [http://www.leefilters.com/lighting/colour-list.html](http://www.leefilters.com/lighting/colour-list.html). They show the spectral energy distribution, but it might be worth getting a swatch (I have the Lee Filters one), before buying a piece. I bought a range of colours years ago (making my own light therapy unit), but have cut one up for devices, spare bits go over torches etc ... I've just raked my swatch out from the depths of the drawer ... you have inspired me to check if the colour I picked for my long gone DIY colour therapy unit could be improved on for the purpose I want it for now....

### Videha — 2016-09-13T10:34:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20w-uv-black-light-advice-please.18416/post-201378>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=201341)
>
> Hi Videha
>
> My computer screen has its own amber glasses .... Its a stage lighting gel, two names I know of are Rosco [https://www.rosco.com/filters/roscolux.cfm#colors](https://www.rosco.com/filters/roscolux.cfm#colors) and Lee Filters. [http://www.leefilters.com/lighting/colour-list.html](http://www.leefilters.com/lighting/colour-list.html). They show the spectral energy distribution, but it might be worth getting a swatch (I have the Lee Filters one), before buying a piece. I bought a range of colours years ago (making my own light therapy unit), but have cut one up for devices, spare bits go over torches etc ... I've just raked my swatch out from the depths of the drawer ... you have inspired me to check if the colour I picked for my long gone DIY colour therapy unit could be improved on for the purpose I want it for now....

Hi Sue, Thanks for the links-briefly looked at them...all a bit over my head at this stage. I do like the idea of your diy light therapy unit- did you get plans off internet,or did some handy person help you? I am interested in the diy idea, sounds really useful...
Did you get good results from yours? Thanks

### Videha — 2016-09-13T10:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20w-uv-black-light-advice-please.18416/post-201379>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=201328)
>
> Videha ,do you know the nm specifications ? My view is thsi in general
> If the light is Uva and a bulb then it is ok. If it is the spirally kind. The round type they sell is a bit weak .
> UV LED I do not recommend as they go very near or even into the blue spectrum
> . What kind of light does your give?Is it really brilliant or just a help when finding your way around?
>
> As far as timing is concered ,have it on when you would have UVA pesent naturally ,that is during the day and maybe an hour in the evening. Use it when you use the PC during the day

\*Reply from the Product Engineer

The band is UVA.
Wavelengths from about 345 to 400 nM are used for "Blacklight" effects (causing many fluorescent objects to glow).
These longer UVA wavelengths emitted by our blacklight and general purpose high pressure mercury lamps are harmless. Shorter UVA wavelengths from 315 to 345 nM (not emitted by our blacklight) are used for suntanning.

That’s correct, the UV 20W globe contains mercury.
In fact all fluorescent lights contain a small amount of mercury which is sealed within the glass tube.

Regards,
Peter Strajner | Product Engineer

### Sue-UK — 2016-09-13T13:08:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20w-uv-black-light-advice-please.18416/post-201380>

> [Videha said:](/goto/post?id=201378)
>
> Hi Sue, Thanks for the links-briefly looked at them...all a bit over my head at this stage. I do like the idea of your diy light therapy unit- did you get plans off internet,or did some handy person help you? I am interested in the diy idea, sounds really useful...
> Did you get good results from yours? Thanks

It was about 18 years ago ..... I was working long hours in a small office with lots of computers and printers, fluorescent lighting,and with that and an underwire bra I developed a skin infection under one of the girls. I had read about colour therapy and tried a standard coloured bulb, not a good idea, it blew and I got a nasty burn from the hot glass .... So DH built a box to house a full spectrum light, mesh in front, and we made up the filters so that they slotted in. But it dawned on me that although it "felt" better, my problem wasn't caused by lack of shining an artificial light source ...... My first subtle energy course introduced me to Becker, I left the job and the unit disappeared to the garage ..... Later complex homeopathy taught me that if all treatment does is relieve a symptom, without removing the cause, it can drive the disease process deeper. A seemingly unrelated problem can later develop. (I now relate the complex homeopathy system to mitochondria, heteroplasmy and the 3 legged stool). The greatest benefit to my skin was leaving the job ....changing my environment ...... I have a commercial light/colour therapy unit, a vertical tanner and an FIR sauna now that I do occasionally use. But only if weather conditions outside make it a better option than doing nothing. Used occasionally its "different", an acute stimulus, used too often I think it becomes "same" chronically, using unnatural light sources and NNemf. The context becomes different ...... It tests my discipline .....  Sitting in a sauna or using the tanner would be soooooo much more comfortable than spending the same time out in the CT tub in the pouring rain ......

# Thread: Optimal from the start on
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-from-the-start-on.18417/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Butters — 2016-09-12T10:35:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-from-the-start-on.18417/post-201340>

I am fresh and greenhorn father since the day before yesterday.
I wonder how to start the optimal journey for my daughter from now on. For nighttime a redlight environment is set for the mother and the child when she must breastfeeding.
But what other things we have to do? I read about grounding the kid through skin to skin contact. Is that a good idea?
Do we have to be careful with the sunlight, because its very warm for september in germany this year? I read that a newborn must not get direct sunlight.

### fitness@home — 2016-09-12T16:07:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-from-the-start-on.18417/post-201345>

Congratulations! Picture please...

### Butters — 2016-09-12T22:04:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-from-the-start-on.18417/post-201359>

No picture here. It is all open to see, sorry.

### Rubicon — 2016-09-13T03:37:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-from-the-start-on.18417/post-201368>

Congratulations!

I haven't done extensive research, but my understanding is that babies born when there's a lot of sunlight typically develop healthier and do better in the long-term. It seems unlikely that babies born in the East African rift zone area while humans were evolving would have been kept from sunlight, and we are likely optimized to receive sunlight from birth.

If I were in your position my instinct would probably be to try and get that kid as much safe sun exposure for the skin type as possible before the sun continues to fall towards a weak winter solstice in Germany. I'd probably look into using artificial UV/IR in winter too. Interested to see what others have to say.

### caroline — 2016-09-13T09:51:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-from-the-start-on.18417/post-201376>

congratulations! how wonderful .....

### NaturalHealthForLife — 2016-10-06T08:01:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-from-the-start-on.18417/post-202375>

Baby carry/snuggle all the time. Mom eats fish/dha foods. Lots of walks with baby outside. Read to and talk to baby. Imagine what Indian tribe would have done with baby while camping outside. No Emf in baby's room. No blue/artificial light. Fresh air open windows.

They used to hang baby's outside the window in a cage playpen to get fresh air (yes!). In Sweden they leave baby strollers outside the store while mom shops.

They are tougher than you think. Skin to skin is good for warming/bonding. Then bundle up and get them outside!

### Jack Kruse — 2016-10-05T23:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keyless-entry-rf-signal.18422/post-202350>

People do not realize how sensitive out mitochondria are.......Falling snow has a sound emission that can be picked up by a detector inside of our skull surrounded by an ocean of CSF, because the act of precession of the hydrogen atom in falling snow makes air move around the particles whose spin was changed. People forget quantum particles always move........they never stop. That resultant circular motion of the top like spin in hydrogen in falling snow is called precession. It turns out the Earth’s magnetic field is “always on” like a quantum particle. Our mitochondria is the detector of this signal. In fact, nearly every magnetically receptive nucleus on Earth, including the hydrogen in every drop of water in the ocean in the clouds, and in snow flakes is quietly precessing away creating the music of nature. The mitochondria in our thalamus listens to this harmony and tunes itself to it! This is one of the ways how we tune our brain to nature and that tune helps fine tune the oscillations in the mitochondrial membranes. It is a wireless network of nature. Snow fall actually has been recorded and it makes noise we can hear if our mitochondrial thalamic detectors have low heteroplasmy rates to pick up these RF frequencies that are altering hydrogen in snowflakes. Our thalamic neurons need good mitochondria and the outer mitochondrial membrane and endoplasmic reticulum must have adequate DHA and eicosanoids in our cell membranes. Mitochondrial function is optimized by optimal melatonin cycles locally in the brain. Our ears are not tuned to this frequency of the sound of snow falling but machines humans have built can pick up these electromagnetic signals; Mother nature built them into our brains to pick them up in thalamic relays that use radio frequency waves. If our ears where to sense these waves it would change the energy and information in those waves, so they by pass our ears so that our brain's mitochondria may use them to tell winter from summer. This is how your mitochondria work in neurons in your brain and sensory organs with your environment way below your sensory tuning abilities. Anytime something is observed or measured the perception of it is altered.

### Rusty — 2023-01-30T08:40:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keyless-entry-rf-signal.18422/post-319294>

Just checked my VW Polo remote control keys using a Trifield TF2. They emit RF only when the button is pressed.

### Anthony Smith — 2023-05-15T19:49:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keyless-entry-rf-signal.18422/post-321372>

Car remote controls (and garage door openers) only put out a signal (a few milli-second pulse) when you push the button.

### ShaneR — 2023-06-24T23:10:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keyless-entry-rf-signal.18422/post-322785>

> [Jeff Power said:](/goto/post?id=201395)
>
> Does anyone know if the keyless entry keys for my 2016 Tahoe emit an RF signal constantly?
>
> I can't find the information on the internet. I am wondering if having my keys in my pocket at now a risk. Or does the car emit the signal and recognizes my keys somehow without my keys giving off an rf?
>
> Thanks!

It won’t emit constantly as it runs on a small battery and so only sends out a periodic signal or a burst any time you press a button on it. If it was constant, you’d be going through batteries all the time.
You could get a faraday pouch to keep the key in while inside your pocket for further protection. Safe Living Technologies make some.

### Marko Pollo — 2023-06-25T03:06:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keyless-entry-rf-signal.18422/post-322788>

# Thread: How much water?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water.18426/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eugenia — 2016-09-14T19:15:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water.18426/post-201437>

Does anyone have a formula about how much water I need per day on Epi-Paleo? I downloaded a phone app you see, and I'd like to program it to notify me to drink water during my waking hours, but the app needs to know how much water in a day (in plain oz, not fl oz I think). I lightly exercise upon waking up at 6:00 AM (30 minutes walking, plus 3 HIIT sprints). Thx!

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-14T23:42:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water.18426/post-201446>

How dehydrating is your environment?

### Eugenia — 2016-09-15T00:14:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water.18426/post-201452>

I work on a wifi-based computer (not laptop) for 3-5 hours a day (about 2.5 ft away from my body), and there's quite some wifi going on from the neighbors' houses (only one neighbor house is a bit too close to ours, the rest of the houses are a bit farther away). But other than that, I don't do cellphone calls much (maybe once a week), and all such devices we have are shoved towards one end of the house, away from the kitchen, family room, and bedroom. Bed is upstairs, not grounded. In addition to my 6:30 AM morning exercise, I go to the nearby park later in the day too, and I get ~1 hour of sun and earthing (on a bamboo mat, although my feet are outside of the mat, and on the ground). In the afternoons, I work on my backyard vegetable and tree garden. I wear blue-blocking glasses for about 1 hour before bed. For such conditions, how much water would I need a day? 43 yo, female. Thx!

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-15T04:42:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water.18426/post-201465>

A lot with that story. You need to check your BUN/creat and trend it. You wont like the resutl I bet

### Eugenia — 2016-09-15T06:10:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water.18426/post-201468>

So, about a gallon a day. Gotcha.

**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cdk29 — 2016-09-15T15:06:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-glutamine-how-to-take.18430/post-201474>

Hi !

I've looked on the forum but didn't found clear sentence about it.

L-glutamine is one of the main supplement involved in Gut RX. But I can't find an argumented protocol :

\- I read on a Jack Kruse's blog comment : 500 to 1500 mg of L glutamine a day. Can it be titrate up ? Or it's a non- trepassing area ?

\- Should it be taken in meal or between ? I've found nothing about it. Some feedback about that ?

Thanks.

### harmony — 2016-09-27T06:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-glutamine-how-to-take.18430/post-201949>

most articles suggest start low and titrate up, it is denatured if taken with hot food or liquid, divided opinions on taking on an empty stomach or with food, buy powder as it works out cheaper, be aware of possible side effects (eg emotional, anxiety or if react to MSG). The following has plenty of comments from people who've taken it [http://scdlifestyle.com/2015/09/l-glutamine-7-dos-and-donts/](http://scdlifestyle.com/2015/09/l-glutamine-7-dos-and-donts/) . Hope someone else can help you from jacks forum perspective...

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-27T15:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-glutamine-how-to-take.18430/post-201968>

you wont find anything about it because you should eat food not pills. Your gut improves as your environment improves.

### omegajosh — 2017-02-10T21:33:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-glutamine-how-to-take.18430/post-208368>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=201968)
>
> you wont find anything about it because you should eat food not pills. Your gut improves as your environment improves.

I've not found that to be true, Jack.

I moved to a little village on the island of Madeira for 5 months in order to biohack sun/low emf/mountain water etc. My gut got worst due to not having access to a very restrictive diet that i can maintain at home. I'm still opento the idea that surface biology rules, but my n=1 (as little as it counts) certainly did not back up the your claims.

# Thread: The cold PUFA connection to grounding effects:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-cold-pufa-connection-to-grounding-effects.18433/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-16T04:30:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-cold-pufa-connection-to-grounding-effects.18433/post-201503>

Cows and horses both evolved outside the tropics. So how do they protect their feet from cold while increasing their ability to use the free electrons from the inverse Hall spin effect in winter?

Inverse Hall spin effect used by plants and animals via grounding. This turns magnetic flux of the Earth's magnetic field into a DC electric current when powerful frequencies of light from the sun are rare. All animals and plants use the DC electric current to regenerate their cells. Becker talked about the Hall effect in the Electric Body, but what he described in Burr's plant data and in his salamander data was in fact the inverse Hall spin effect which has now been found to exist by modern physics. So how does this work in animals who represent the two main animal family survivors in the KT event when food was not plentiful. So how do animals manage?

Both cows and horses have a high concentration of PUFAs in their feet in wintertime. Did you know that? Have you ever seen a horse with 37º C hoofs wintertime? No, never. Also nautical compass houses used to have hoof oil as a dampening liquid, that is PUFA from horse hoofs. So even the same animal can have different fat composition in different parts of the animal depending on the climate, temperature, and incident light coming to its retina. How? The non linear signaling is lost because bright intense light inhibits these programs in animals outside the tropics. This is why tropical oils are rarely PUFA's and why they are most saturated. (coconut/palm oil)

Look at the white swans in the Stockholm Ström in the middle of Stockholm wintertime. They swim in the 1º C water with their bare feet having the same temperature as the water. Then they jump up on the ice when it’s -10º C air temperature and their wet feet freeze stuck to the ice surface. Then the swan has two options, increase the blood flow to the feet and melt the ice around the feet and freeze to death or wait for the fire brigade to come and saw off a piece of ice with the swan and then transport the iced swan to the fire station to thaw the ice. Usually the swan chooses the second option. In cold horses and swan can still activate their thyroid to work without bright intense light. How?

The local mitochondria in thyroid also pay attention to the sun's signals so you dont have to unless you bury the sun, or live outside the tropics. Most modern humans break both rules. Hence why their thyroid thermostat is broken. But the horses and swans here have a lesson for us. The more electrons in cell membranes from any physical source, the better living things can handle hot and cold. Why? Not only does the sun give us light energy, but grounding gives living things free electrons too. We can add electrons from sunlight via the photoelectric effect and grounding by the inverse Hall magnetic spin effect.

DHA. a PUFA in humans, is heavily represented in our cell membrane responds, "I know this and this is why I capture them with my pi-electron cloud and transform their energy." DHA assimilation increases in tissues in cold weather. In warm weather you do not need as much because the sun can power your life. Wintertime is a different environment. Its cold and food is sparse.
Electrons in motion = electricity = and electric fields in motion induce magnetic flux = life uses magnetic flux from many places to control life within the Earth’s magnetic field. There are two sides of the mammalian battery in humans that use sunlight and magnetic flux that can be charged or slowly discharged based upon how we chose to live our life. Mammals are capable of transforming magnetic flux into a DC electric current using the tensegrity system’s piezoelectric abilities, using semiconductors capable of using the inverse spin Hall effect. These changes affect cell water and blood plasma in our extremities to get the message far and wide in the system of life. This is why your leptin receptor in your hypothalamus is an accountant for electrons and why your thyroid mitochondria and the mitochondria in your feet are quantum measureing devices that communicate via the Q-cycle. The Q-cycle is capable of splitting light frequencies build quantum radar systems that allow the mitochondria in your feet and thyroid and brain to communicate using entangles photos. This is how your brain's leptin receptor measures your quantum yield of the electrons you collect in the environment. In winter, it responds with a brisk free T3 output if the mammal is propelry grounding. Wild mammals do but modern humans don't. If your T3 is too low you need sun or grounding to make up the deficit, if its too high you live on the equator and need some CT. You are what you absorb repel and emit. This is far more true than "you are what you eat”.

LIFE IS A LIGHT BULB DESIGNED TO BE LIT BY THE SUN AND SCREWED INTO ITS SOCKET CALLED EARTH.

Don’t your find it interesting how the human anatomy highlights – full spectrum sunlight……….
All the human body is a tube. We are actually “hollow” on the inside with all our cavities filled with different fluids that act like a filament. The sheath that separates our body from all the things we stuff in our tube is called the gut “lumen.”
Isn’t it cool that there is a “light shaft” running through our bodies? Have you ever stopped and thought about that aspect of your being?

Angular momentum of photons is just one of the ways electrons are linked to magnetism. Magnetic moments of proteins and water act perpendicular to both the electric field and the direction of the angular momentum of light. Angular momentum can be described as how light turns or rotates into a medium when it interacts. Magnetic flux can be turned into a DC electric current using the angular momentum of light. This is the basis of the inverse Hall spin effect. This effect is the key scientific principle behind how grounding works. Why is this important for animals who are related to eutherian mammals? Long ago, 65 million years ago, two animal classes made it through the last extinction event. One was therapod dinosaurs (swans) and the other was eutherian mammals. (horses) The key that linked them to survival to this event was both had excessive mitochondrial capacity. Therapod dinosaurs (swans) needed their extra mito capacity to disconnect from the Earth's magnetic flux to fly. Eutherian mammals gained their capacity to disconnect from the sun for 6 months to hibernate through the winter without food. Our deep ancestors are these eutherian mammals. This implies deep within human mitochondria is an innate built in design to use angular momentum and magnetic flux to replace food and optimize mitochondrial function via grounding. Gorunding worse via the inverse Hall magnetic spin effect of electrons and works with PUFA's in cell membranes in winter when UV light is sparse to absent. I have a deep sense grounding is critically important to our species who live outside the tropics still because modern humans who disconnect from the Earth and sun most tend to get peripheral neuropathy in the distal extremities when wearing insulating shoes in winter and not making that critical connection to walk upon the Earth with PUFA increases in their feet and legs. It seems no modern physics is just beginning to understand that light has a lot more abilities built into it than we believe based upon current electromagnetic teachings. [http://phys.org/news/2016-05-physicists.html#jCp](http://phys.org/news/2016-05-physicists.html#jCp)

Grounding to Earth specifically makes your hydrogen atoms precess more in you to create more magnetic flux in you mitochondrial surfaces to shrink the scale of physics possible (QED/QCD) to allow you to make more energy while rely on less ATP from the ATPase. This is a very similiar to the mechanisms that control sleep. The Earth’s magnetic field never turns off. The electric power grid oscillates and is not constant. This is where the problem lies with grounding appliances and why I don’t like them. Our physical bodies have many antennae in our sensory pathways that lead all lead to our thalamus in the brain. Our thalamus is where the alpha waves are produced in the brain that yoke to the magnetic dynamo and to circadian wave forms from the eye. Those alpha waves created deep in the brain match the frequency of the Schumann resonance from the Earth’s magnetic field. In this way, the thalamus acts as a “tuning fork” for incoming sensory signals from our sensory receptors. This allows for integration from the environment that are tuned in to a variety of frequencies. Those environmental signals, tuning our thalamus should always be the sun and the magnetic field and not the power grid or a fake light bulb. These sensory portals all wind up affecting mitochondrial surfaces on the cristae and inside the matrix to alter the physical scale of what is possible in a mitochondria. These mechanisms act a a dial or knob for the mitochondria in determining how we may handle electrons and protons from food that are excited by sunlight. Feynman’s interview describing waves all around us is quite instructional to my view point here. [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FjHJ7FmV0M4](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.youtube.com%2Fwatch%3Fv%3DFjHJ7FmV0M4&h=iAQFyQ5eC)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-16T04:33:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-cold-pufa-connection-to-grounding-effects.18433/post-201504>

Most nonlinear optical effects can be described using classical electromagnetic fields, and, in fact, the initial theory of nonlinear optics was done with classical fields. The problem for biology and medicine is that they study everything in biochemistry using a linear methodology. This implies that the published data loses all non linear apsects of life. This is especially true in the Q-cycle of mitochondria. The Q-cycle that shuttles electrons from cytochrome 1 to 3 and 4 is a beam splitter of light. When a beam splits several non linear quantum mechanical things occur. When these electromagnetic field of mitochondria are quantized we should except a number of new effects emerge. Those effects are all non linear apsects of light. Life uses these to do the unique things it does. Quantizing fields are necessary if we want to describe fields that originate from spontaneous emission of light from proteins. This is precisely what happens in the Q-cycle. For example, in a process known as spontaneous parametric down conversion, a beam of light at one frequency, the pump, produces a beam at half the original frequency, creating the optical signal Why is this critical to understand? The secondary beam is a result of spontaneous emission from these respiratory proteins and can lead to novel new phenomena of light that would not be measured by linear methodologies. Moreover, the quantum properties of the down-converted beam are novel as I described to my members in a recent webinar on quantum entanglement and quantum radar. How so? Its photons are produced in pairs, one pump photon disappearing to produce, simultaneously, two signal photons. This leads to strong correlations between the split photons from the signal beam, and photons produced in this way can be quantum mechanically entangled. In addition, the signal beam can have smaller phase fluctuations than is possible with classical light. Both of these properties have made light produced by parametric down conversion useful for applications in the field of quantum information processing. You mitochondria is like a deli slicer for light in your cell and most biologist do not even know enough to measure it much less be able ot explain its effects in cell biology. [http://www.jbc.org/…/ear…/2003/06/26/jbc.M305461200.full.pdf](http://www.jbc.org/content/early/2003/06/26/jbc.M305461200.full.pdf)

Also, does anyone notice that the body seems warmer while barefoot than it would seem if you were outside under the same conditions but with shoes on? That's definitely the case for me. And if so, is a theory believable that the relative body comfort is all due to the electrons?

### Glenn A — 2019-10-15T18:29:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-cold-pufa-connection-to-grounding-effects.18433/post-280484>

> [Bob Stirling said:](/goto/post?id=261246)
>
> Yes the body became more thermogenic. Also the native people wore less clothes in the same conditions.

In confirmation of your finding of "more thermogenic" I find that it's probably partly due to improved blood flow due to vasodilation brought about by: first, penetration of UVA light into the skin; second, delivery of Nitric Oxide (NO) brought on by the UVA activating an "intracutaneous, non-enzymatic reaction" (which is really just UVA exposure causing nitrite decomposition producing NO).

It makes sense that improved blood flow into the skin from the interior of the body will tend to keep the skin temperature higher than will slower blood flow. We mostly know that NO causes a reduction in blood pressure by causing vasodilation, but we ignore the fact that the dilation also allows increased flow. This may be the secret to why our extremities "seem" warmer when grounded outside, especially on cold mornings: _they actually are warmer_.

[https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIRCRESAHA.109.207019](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIRCRESAHA.109.207019)

While the study worked with whole-body UVA irradiation, I think the NO will circulate and have a full-body effect once released into the plasma, even if it was released in only one's arms and face that are exposed to the sun's UVA radiation. This can explain why my whole body feels warmer when grounded and exposed to morning sunlight. It's not just my feet standing in snow that are surviving better. It's not just the grounding, though that should help speed circulation by reducing clumping of red blood cells. But as time passes, there should be additional NO released within exposed cutaneous tissue, affording additional vasodilation that brings further improvement in circulation volume.

For more on the protective effect of nitrites (against excess UVA) within cutaneous tissue, and how the protective chemistry results in NO, which does a lot more than just relax blood vessels:

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3565258/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3565258/)

# Thread: Omega-3 PUFA Deficiency Abolishes Endocannabinoid-Linked Neuronal Functions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omega-3-pufa-deficiency-abolishes-endocannabinoid-linked-neuronal-functions.18436/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JediMindTricks — 2016-09-17T13:40:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omega-3-pufa-deficiency-abolishes-endocannabinoid-linked-neuronal-functions.18436/post-201544>

Hello folks, I find this article very interesting as someone who suffers from social anxiety which came about after having a panic attack while smoking Cannabis when I was younger. The article links n-3 PUFA-deficiency with increased anxiety and impaired social exploration behaviors:

The endocannabinoid signaling system is an important route through which long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LC-PUFAs) affect behavior, mood, responses to stress, anxiety, memory, energy balance and other systems. Cannabinoid receptors and the substances that activate or antagonize them (endocannabinoids) are under intensive investigation for their involvement in substance abuse, addiction, impaired neurotransmission, memory, emotion, obesity and many other situations. Cannabinoid receptors are widely distributed in human tissues, but are especially concentrated in the brain. These receptors regulate synaptic plasticity, which is the ability of neurons to alter the strength of their response. Plasticity also results from changes in the number of receptors on a synapse.

The cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2 are coupled to G-proteins in the neuronal cell membrane through which they stimulate or inhibit other processes. In laboratory mice, stimulation of the endocannabinoid system increases feeding, enhances the reward aspects of eating and promotes lipogenesis. In contrast, a novel antagonist of CB1 was reported to reduce food intake and body weight. In obese men, higher levels of the endocannabinoid 2-arachidonoyl-glycerol (2-AG) are directly related to the level of intra-abdominal adiposity.

The two best known ligands for the cannabinoid receptors are derived from the omega-6 (n-6) LC-PUFA arachidonic acid (ARA). These are anandamide (N-arachidonoylethanolamine) and 2-AG. Endocannabinoid levels are affected by the relative amounts of ARA and DHA in the diet. Dietary supplementation with ARA and DHA led to significantly increased levels of anandamide and its DHA-form as well as higher levels of the ARA- and DHA-2-acylglycerols in several regions of the neonatal piglet brain. Piglets not consuming these LC-PUFAs had significantly lower concentrations of these substances compared with the supplemented and sow-milk-fed piglets.

Mice fed n-3 PUFA-deficient diets had greater amounts of 2-AG in brain than mice fed sufficient n-3 PUFAs. Feeding the animals a DHA-rich fish oil diet was accompanied by a decrease in brain 2-AG.

Others have reported that mice fed a DHA-enriched diet had significantly increased levels of docosahexaenoylethanolamine in the brain, with correspondingly lower levels of anandamide. Dietary DHA did not significantly affect the brain content of ARA or any of the 6 glycerol-ester endocannabinoids measured, except for eicosapentaenoylglycerol.

Several questions emerge from these and other studies. One addresses the functional links between dietary LC-PUFA consumption and the production of different endocannabinoid species. Another is the functional differences between the various endocannabinoids, especially the ethanolamine and glycerol types, which respond differently to dietary n-3 LC-PUFA changes. In this report, a French team of investigators addressed the functional effects of lifelong n-3 PUFA deficiency by examining synaptic plasticity in the prefrontal cortex of mice.

The researchers fed female mice throughout gestation and lactation 6%-fat diets based on African peanut oil, deficient in n-3 PUFAs and rich in linoleic acid. They used a rapeseed oil diet rich in alpha-linolenic acid as a control. The offspring consumed the same diets after weaning throughout life and were studied from ages 6 to 16 weeks. The investigators examined the fatty acids in the brain, endocannabinoid-dependent and independent synaptic plasticity, cannabinoid receptor coupling, circulating endocannabinoid levels and synaptic currents and behaviors related to the diet-induced changes in cannabinoid receptor function. They focused on the prefrontal cortex (Figure 1), the region associated with executive tasks and rewards, emotional behavior and depression. All evaluations were conducted in the n-3-PUFA-deficient offspring of the mothers fed the n-3-PUFA-deficient diet.

The investigators first established that the n-3-PUFA-deficient diet led to significant decreases in DHA, n-3 docosapentaenoic acid and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) in the whole brain and prefrontal cortex, which were accompanied by significant increases in n-6 docosapentaenoic acid and n-6 tetraenoic acid.

To assess synaptic plasticity, they applied tetanic stimulation at 10 Hz to slices of prefrontal cortex from animals fed the n-3 PUFA adequate and deficient diets. Tetanic stimulation is the delivery of very rapid electrical stimuli to prefrontal cortex synapses that induces synaptic responses. In this experiment, tetanic stimulation induced normal endocannabinoid-mediated long-term depression responses in the pyramidal neurons in the prelimbic area of the prefrontal cortex in animals consuming the n-3 PUFA adequate diet. The long-term depression is a sustained reduction in the response of neurons which can last several hours or longer. In the n-3 PUFA deficient animals, long-term depression was completely abolished (Figure 2). A similar response was observed for the nucleus accumbens area (Figure 1), which has also been linked to reward and pleasure as well as mood disorders. The prelimbic area of the prefrontal cortex communicates with the nucleus accumbens via excitatory projections. These observations suggest that n-3 PUFA deficiency abolished endocannabinoid-mediated synaptic plasticity in different regions of the brain.

The researchers also investigated whether the effects of n-3 PUFA deficiency were observed in other forms of synaptic plasticity, such as those mediated via AMPA and NMDA glutamate receptors, which are also involved in long-term synaptic plasticity. Results from these experiments revealed no differences between the n-3 PUFA sufficient and deficient groups. These findings plus the demonstration that n-3 PUFA deficiency had no effect on downstream transduction pathways suggested that the observations on endocannabinoid-mediated synaptic plasticity were specific to this type of synaptic process and were not a generalized effect.

To determine whether n-3 PUFA deficiency affected the ability of the cannabinoid receptor CB1 to couple with its effector G-proteins, the investigators used a CB1 agonist to assess presynaptic function. They observed that the agonist inhibited synaptic transmission in the n-3 PUFA sufficient animals in a manner similar to that seen in chow-fed mice, but that inhibition of synaptic transmission was greatly reduced in the n-3 PUFA deficient animals. When the researchers stimulated CB1 receptor binding to its isotope-labeled G-protein binding protein, they found that binding was significantly reduced in specific layers of the prelimbic prefrontal cortex in the n-3 PUFA deficient animals compared with the n-3 PUFA sufficient animals. Receptor binding was not different in the motor cortex andCB1 expression was not affected by the dietary deficiency.

Circulating levels of the two major endocannabinoids were similar in both n-3 PUFA groups. As this observation contrasts with previous results, the authors suggested that homeostatic adaptations may have occurred in the n-3 PUFA deficient animals or high concentrations of endocannabinoids near synapses may not be accurately measured with bulk measurements.

Finally, the research team investigated whether there were behavioral changes in the two groups of animals that were related to their n-3 PUFA status. Using a forced swimming test, a model of depressive behavior, they observed that the n-3-deficient mice spent more time immobile and less time swimming compared with the n-3 PUFA sufficient animals. They also observed other behaviors in the deficient animals that were indicative of impaired exploratory behavior and greater anxiety, including fewer social explorations, more litter-scratching, less time spent in the center of the exploratory arena and a greater tendency to remain close to the arena wall.

In summary, these experiments demonstrated that n-3 PUFA deficient diets:

• led to decreased DHA, EPA and docosapentaenoic acid levels in the whole brain and prefrontal cortex;
• abolished endocannabinoid-dependent synaptic plasticity, but not other types of synaptic plasticity;
• desensitized and uncoupled CB1 binding without affecting the expression of CB1 receptors;
• may have altered endocannabinoid-CB1 binding at synapses, which diminishes endocannabinoid synaptic plasticity;
• were associated with increased anxiety and impaired social exploration behaviors.

In discussing these findings, the authors suggested that Western diets high in n-6 PUFAs and low in n-3 PUFAs, especially the latter, might create conditions where CB1 function in the brain is sufficiently impaired so that mood disorders develop more readily. Ligands for these receptors differ by type and the class of PUFA from which they are derived. For example, animals fed diets containing ARA and DHA had multi-fold increases in the endocannabinoids in the brain, while supplementation with DHA enhanced the production of n-3-PUFA-derived endocannabinoids at the expense of those derived from ARA. Over-activation of the endocannabinoid system, especially the CB1 receptors, is associated with obesity and reduced insulin sensitivity. On the other hand, antagonists of the CB1 receptor are associated with reduced food intake and lower abdominal adiposity. Further, a clinical trial of a drug that blocks CB1 receptors designed to control food intake in obese individuals was halted because of the development of psychiatric problems. These results support the evidence that dysregulation of the endocannabinoid system is associated with mood disorders and other brain functions. Further elucidation of the different effects of n-3 and n-6 LC-PUFAs in the regulation of the endocannabinoid system is likely to be increasingly important.

Source, Graphs and Figures: [Articles on Health Nutrition Research : Benefits of Fish, Omega-3s, Fish Oil and Polyunsaturated Fats](http://www.fatsoflife.com/article.php?nid=1&edition=this&id=1837&issueid=85)

# Thread: nnEMF exposure linked to will power?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nnemf-exposure-linked-to-will-power.18437/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx

# Thread: Jack Kruse - The Quilt - Podcast of Audio Blogs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kruse-the-quilt-podcast-of-audio-blogs.18444/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 59
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Liam Taylor — 2016-09-19T14:02:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kruse-the-quilt-podcast-of-audio-blogs.18444/post-201594>

Update November 2020:

I'm pleased to say I am continuing to record Dr Kruse's publicly available blogs. Main takeaways:

-   Audio quality is much improved
-   I have started a podcast feed for this - YouTube can be a great service, but unless you have premium, you can't listen whilst on airplane mode or power off the screen.
-   Time 5 is live today

Request: It would be great if anyone with any music production experience would be so kind as to provide intro and outro music. I don't really hold the skillset for that, and it does help improve the impression of a podcast for the listener.

RSS feed - [https://pinecast.com/feed/jack-kruse-the-quilt](https://pinecast.com/feed/jack-kruse-the-quilt)
Spotify - [https://open.spotify.com/show/0ajL41JcuzZIsfb5vSxYAW?si=8L58VtLPRtiuB-4-RYkM1Q](https://open.spotify.com/show/0ajL41JcuzZIsfb5vSxYAW?si=8L58VtLPRtiuB-4-RYkM1Q)
Apple - [https://podcasts.apple.com/gb/podcast/jack-kruse-the-quilt/id1541856764](https://podcasts.apple.com/gb/podcast/jack-kruse-the-quilt/id1541856764)

If you notice any random whistling sounds when listening then please let me know - these are my markers for editing when I have made a mistake.

### Liam Taylor — 2016-09-19T14:35:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kruse-the-quilt-podcast-of-audio-blogs.18444/post-201597>

> [Snowie said:](/goto/post?id=201596)
>
> Great idea!
>
> How would you envisage others being able to help?

Thanks for the feedback. I think that YouTube probably has the ability to create collaborative playlists, so if others recorded readings of blogs, we could (hopefully) form a collaborative playlist with our variety of recordings.

### Rubicon — 2016-09-19T18:19:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kruse-the-quilt-podcast-of-audio-blogs.18444/post-201604>

Nice, I also like listening to blogs. I've used the Pocket app to have machine-read text, but the pronunciation is often in error and the voice/pacing is not the best: [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...sed-to-listen-to-the-blogs.17361/#post-185911](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/apps-that-can-be-used-to-listen-to-the-blogs.17361/#post-185911)

I wonder if the Kruse team has ever considered hiring a professional narrator to create audio versions of the blogs? Upwork seems to have people that provide this service for $25-$100+ per hour: [https://www.upwork.com/o/profiles/browse/?q=narration](https://www.upwork.com/o/profiles/browse/?q=narration)

Personally, I would love it if I could fire up my podcast app, find the Jack Kruse "Blog Cast", and download & listen to any blog. I'd be willing to contribute to make that happen.

### Joeakim — 2016-09-19T20:53:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kruse-the-quilt-podcast-of-audio-blogs.18444/post-201614>

> [Liam Taylor said:](/goto/post?id=201594)
>
> \- I have just uploaded a video to YouTube of an audio recording of the time 1 blog.
>
> I have been putting off reading a lot of the blogs due to an adversity to reading on a screen. For a while I have wanted the option of listening to Jack's blogs rather than read. So because they weren't available, I thought that the right thing to do would be to do it myself. Jack gave me the okay last week.
>
> I am sorry that I've done it via YouTube than a podcast. Ideally it would have been a podcast so you didn't have to keep the screen on or be online and connected to access it. Unfortunately I just can't afford the podcast hosting fees right now. So rather than do nothing, I thought recordings on YouTube would at least be a start.
>
> I hope others find this useful and think I should do more. I am open to feedback on the idea and my narration. This is my first time narrating something. I hope to improve my style for the next one.
>
> I had hoped that others might want to get involved so that we can record more and also have a variety of voices. Please let me know if you are interested and we can pick blogs to read.

Thank So much for this! I was actually was going to post a request to Jack Kruse to attach audio as well.

Thanks again.

Liam... much thanx... need to put these files to listen in transit and also in CT tub. Much thanks for such work... it serves a need. Much appreciated. I have put together the blogs in files which can be print and bound to get myself off computer for this very reason... you are helping many learn.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-21T02:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kruse-the-quilt-podcast-of-audio-blogs.18444/post-201677>

> [Liam Taylor said:](/goto/post?id=201652)
>
>
> Time 2 - almost two hours!

Time is built by wakefulness. Every object appears to have a definite position, a definite momentum, a definite measured value, and a definite time of occurrence in wakefulness. Is the same true in sleep? Why does every living thing sleep? Might the reason be so it cannot carefully observe the quantum state of life? This one may surprise some who follow scientism and reductionism. In 1977, physicists E. C. G. Sudarshan and B. Misra studying quantum mechanics discovered that the dynamical evolution of motion of a non linear quantum system can be hindered and even inhibited through observation of the system. Since humans are non linear quantum beings this maybe why our eyes and minds are designed not to observe the smallest things in our cells. Maybe Mother Nature does not want us to see so we know? The arrow of time is disrupted just by looking at things closely or measuring them.

### Josh Rosenthal — 2016-09-19T19:32:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughs-about-being-present.18445/post-201607>

this mornings CT session brought this thought to mind...

when talking about staying in now, being present... this may just represent how well one integrates their sensory inputs. Observation, sensing touch, feeling breath, and hearing when all being taken in force you into the present/ the now. When you ignore all the wonderful information happening around you/inside you... you let your mind wander into the past and future.

Today I will put more intention on integrating all my senses.

Good day!

# Thread: Seasonal Adaptations: Experiences & Advice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-adaptations-experiences-advice.18446/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kris90 — 2016-09-19T21:20:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-adaptations-experiences-advice.18446/post-201615>

This one goes out to those in the northern and southern hemispheres.

My understanding is, the further away from the equator you live, the more variance in season will occur. Everywhere on earth gets 50/50 light and darkness over a 365 day period (Earth's full revolution around the sun). At the equator it is 50/50 year round. Correct me if I am wrong...

Now, for those with Spring, Summer, Autumn and Winter, when you are "optimal" (no need to heal, just maintain good health), do you allow yourself to warm adapt in Summer and cold adapt in Winter, or do you try to stay somewhat cold adapted year round? The reason I ask, is because I know that being warm adapted throughout winter (i.e. using artificial lighting, heating and consuming lots of carbs, aka living the modern lifestyle) can be quite detrimental to health and this long term life style is not optimal, especially given the mismatch it creates.

Now on the contrary, what if "special people" like all of us here who have this awareness decide to live cold adapted, or kind of a mix between cold and warm adapted year-round to still achieve that balance, but occasionally creating a mismatch. For example, practicing CT year round, eating keto-ish (going in and out of ketosis, with no particular alignment with the seasons, i.e. sometimes being in strict ketosis during summer, and sometimes eating carbs here and there in winter) and also fasting year-round (I know fasting is more in tune with winter).

Is there any harm in living more cold-adapted in Summer, but still getting as much sun as possible? I would almost think combining cold with warm/light would create some kind of synergy (sensitizing the effects of the light/sun).

Let's say for example someone adopts the principals of: limit blue light & EMF (as well as wear blue-blockers, and lots of grounding), eat low carb/high fat w/ lots of DHA (but not keto), incorporate daily CT protocol, exercise, intermittent fasting (only consuming food in presence of daylight) and 8 hours of sleep year round irregardless of season. Would this be detrimental, because we are not TRULY adapting to the environment, or would this be ok since we are avoiding the overstimulation period of summer, thus eliminating the need for a "repairative/restorative" winter? Basically trying to be somewhat adapted to both warm and cold year round, but always focusing on these little biohacks to offset any damage.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-19T23:29:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-adaptations-experiences-advice.18446/post-201620>

is it always just about latitude? nope

# Thread: One of the best anti-inflammatories for patients? CURCUMIN
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-of-the-best-anti-inflammatories-for-patients-curcumin.18447/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-20T03:05:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-of-the-best-anti-inflammatories-for-patients-curcumin.18447/post-201629>

Shellfish broths with curcumin and pepper should be your go to medication. Curcumin undergoes massive protein fluorescence naturally. So this means it scavenges excessive bio-photon release from stressed cells. All cells in an inflamamtory state release massive amounts ot ELF-UV. One of the major challenge in medicine is to find alternatives treatments for inflammatory conditions like arthrtitis and cancer with "a chemotherapy" or pill that is safe and clinically efficacious. Tumeric has a low toxicity profile and this makes it something that everyone should consider. Many practitioner's do not have a good backround in the bio-physical aspects of disease so they may not know that curcumin (diferuloylmethane), a naturally occurring flavinoid derived from the rhizome of Curcuma longa is an ideal candidate because of how it sops up UV light because it really absorbs it well. If you add some of the shells of shellfish and black pepper to the mix in seafood bone broths you make it even stronger anti-inflammatory. Chitosan and curcumin are a big part of the Epi-paleo Rx. Curcumin is fluorophore protein for those who don't know. In vitro and in vivo preclinical studies have shown its inhibitory anticancer, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antiproliferative, and proapoptotic activities in mitochondria which lower inflammation. The multiple mechanisms of the anti-inflammatory effect of curcumin putatively include down-regulating the expression of gene products such as nuclear factor-κB, growth suppression, inducing apoptosis, and modulating various signal transduction pathways and the expression of many inflammatory genes and oncogenes. Based on resonance light scattering and UV-visible absorption spectroscopic analysis, mechanism studies have suggested that the highly enhanced fluorescence of curcumin has resulted from synergic effects of a favorable hydrophobic microenvironment provided by albumin and chitosan to facilitate efficient intermolecular energy transfer between albumin and chitosan in the blood plasma. Right now the latest science is that albumin seems to bind to curcumin through its hydrogen bonding network, which causes the protein conformation to convert from β-folded helix to α-helix. This radically changes the Faraday effect in the local environment and improves the local electric and magnetic fields in tissues with inflammation. Curcumin can combine with the the albumen chitosan complex through its center carbonyl carbon, and the chitosan from the shells of seafood can play a key role in promoting the energy transfer process by shortening the distance between albumen and chitosan to transfer the light energy without shorting out the cells microenvironment. Bio-physics is remarkable when take the time to understand it.

New research suggests that ibuprofen can lead to damage to the heart muscle, and that turmeric is actually a more effective pain reliever than ibuprofen.

According to various sources, the best turmeric pain relief recipe is to take a tablespoon of turmeric powder, the juice of one squeezed lemon, and a cup of honey. Stir well the mixture with a wooden spoon, and add a teaspoon of the mixture to a cup of tea.

Drink this turmeric tea three times a day, and you will pain relief within a few hours.

If you drink this tea, you will also enjoy all of the other health benefits of this famous spice. Turmeric is a part of many recipes in India and many other places across the globe.

A recent study published in the Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine suggests that turmeric is extremely effective in treating osteoarthritis.

The study noted in the ACM journal lasted for 6 weeks and included 109 knee osteoarthritis patients. Some patients received 800 mg of Ibuprofen, while the rest received 2,000 mg of turmeric daily.

Researchers then measured the amount pain during physical activities and exercise such as climbing stairs, walking, and bicycling.

It turned out that the patients who consumed turmeric showed far better pain relief, so the study confirmed that turmeric is a much more effective and safer option to treat pain than Ibuprofen. [http://www.health.harvard.edu/blog/...ase-heart-attack-and-stroke-risk-201507138138](http://www.health.harvard.edu/blog/fda-strengthens-warning-that-nsaids-increase-heart-attack-and-stroke-risk-201507138138)

[https://www.researchgate.net/public...\_spices\_turmeric\_and\_ginger\_on\_heat\_treatment](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/234002731_Change_in_antioxidant_activity_of_spices_turmeric_and_ginger_on_heat_treatment)

Dude, it's because of the light that's stored in it that it has such fantastic medicinal benefits: which is why vit a/b1/b2/b3 and b12 are such a hit...
[http://chemistry.about.com/od/glowi...s-Glow-Under-a-Black-or-Ultraviolet-Light.htm](http://chemistry.about.com/od/glowingprojects/ss/What-Materials-Glow-Under-a-Black-or-Ultraviolet-Light.htm)
[http://chemistry.about.com/od/glowi...s-Glow-Under-a-Black-or-Ultraviolet-Light.htm](http://chemistry.about.com/od/glowingprojects/ss/What-Materials-Glow-Under-a-Black-or-Ultraviolet-Light.htm)
and, if you have kids, here's a fun experiment:
**How to make your own fluorescent dye**
You can make your own fluorescent dye easily, after a trip to the kitchen for some powdered turmeric spice, and the bathroom for some rubbing alcohol.

Put a teaspoon of turmeric into a glass, and add a few tablespoons of alcohol. Fold a paper towel into quarters to make a funnel, and filter the liquid into another glass. The photo below shows the liquid in a tiny glass vial, lit by our UV LEDs.

You can use a cotton swab to write things with your new ink. It will appear yellow on the paper, and bright yellow-green when lit with ultraviolet light.

# Thread: Tricia's Journal #2
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tricias-journal-2.18452/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tricia McVey — 2016-09-20T19:38:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tricias-journal-2.18452/post-201659>

Its been 9 months since I last posted! Some changes that I have implemented are:

I have not worked in ICU since June this year. This was hard to let go, I am a critical care nurse at heart but it allows for me to work in a different department with more flexible hours and I have more opportunity to get outside during my shift and soak up some sun and put my feet in the water fountain.

I finally got myself a pair of blutechs and they have made a big difference with working under the fluorescent lights and computer screens. I love to be able to just take them off when I am outside.

Blue blockers at night, check. Wifi off at night, check.

Unless I have to be at work by 7am I am on my deck and grass to look into the morning sun and ground for as long as I can. I have spend more time outside in my backyard this year I think than ever before and Im loving it.

Drinking enough water at work and at home and switched to Tom's fluoride free toothpaste.

I started the leptin reset a couple weeks ago, I still need to loose some weight. I never eat in the mornings and this has been the most challenging to eat when I wasn't hungry.

Things I have not done~

Still taking birth control pills~ post tubal ligation ovulation pain is horrible.

Still taking 50 mcg of levothyroxine. Been on since 2012 which I looked back and at that time I had spent 11 years full time in ICU, my Vitamin D was 25 and I was exhausted and had gained weight for the first time in my life. Even post two babies I got back to my pre pregnancy weight no problem. I asked my Dr to check my thyroid which showed hypo, I believe my level was in the high 3's not too bad but when he started me on the levo, I did feel better energy wise.

No major medical issues ~ I have developed plantar fasciitis in my left heel, self diagnosed lol. I get achy joints in my hips and just generalized body aches in the evening especially after a long day at work standing and lifting patients, transferring to gurneys etc.

Weight loss is a big goal right now. I want to loose 30 lbs or more. I am 5'8 and should not be in the 180's ... my weight the majority of my life was in the 130's or less. I have not implemented CT, yet.

Just got a bunch of labs done last week, would love some feedback.

Vitamin D is 63.7 all from the sun, no supplement. It was about 43 last November.
HS-CRP is 0.5 mg/DL my doctor suggested crestor for this which I refused
CRP is also 0.5

Total cholesterol 210
triglycerides 115
HDL 57
LDL 130
VLDL 23
cholest/hdl ratio 4 ( I have requested a VAP 2 times and one is supposed to be pending)

HgA1c 5.2
TSH 1.92 ( I wish I asked for a free T4)

Omega -3 (EPA+DHA ) index~ 4.6 ( reference range is 1.4-4.9 )
omega-6/omega -3 ratio ~ 5.8 ( reference range is 5.7-21.3) I'm guessing this is good?
EPA/Arachidonic acid ration 0.2 (reference range is 0.2 or less)
Arachnoid Acid 7.4% (reference range is 5.2 -12.9)
EPA 1.25 (reference range 0.2-1.5)
DHA 3.4% (reference range 1.2-3.9)

HGB 14.5
HCT 43.1
PLT 282
NA 139
K 3.7
Co2 25
BUN 14
Cr 0.82

Jack, if you would be able to give me any feedback on my labs, in particular the HS-CRP would be appreciated. I want to taper down my levy but thought maybe I should get some weight off before doing so and sticking hard with the leptin reset and implementing some CT.

Thanks !!

Tricia

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-20T19:51:29+0300

### Tricia McVey — 2016-09-20T22:15:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tricias-journal-2.18452/post-201670>

Okay. Do you agree with this HS-CRP risk factor range? It's not a lab I see very often at work and not sure how to gauge my result.

**Interpretation**
Relative risk of future cardiovascular events based on hs-CRP testing is estimated as follows:[\[1\]](http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/2094831-reference)

-   Low risk: CRP < 1.0 mg/L
-   Intermediate risk: CRP 1.0-3.0 mg/L
-   High risk: CRP > 3.0 mg/L

Acute inflammation is a CRP greater than 10.0 mg/L.

Relative risk in the high-risk group is estimated to be twice that in the low-risk group.

Thank you again,

Tricia

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-21T02:57:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tricias-journal-2.18452/post-201678>

HS crp is decent. Needs no statin.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-21T05:37:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tricias-journal-2.18452/post-201682>

Somebody pointed out Tricia that you conflated the units.......you need to convert them all to make accurate comparisons. You wrote that your HS-CRP is 0.5 mg/DL. I am not sure if this is a typo or it is the units it was reported in. Check it. Yours are mg/DL and below it is mg/L so that is a huge correction factor. When I first read them I thought they were all the same.......and they were not. If these are correct then that 0.5mg/dl is very high and a real problem.

**Interpretation**
Relative risk of future cardiovascular events based on hs-CRP testing is estimated as follows:[\[1\]](http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/2094831-reference)

-   Low risk: CRP < 1.0 mg/L
-   Intermediate risk: CRP 1.0-3.0 mg/L
-   High risk: CRP > 3.0 mg/L

### Joe Gavin — 2016-09-23T22:12:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tricias-journal-2.18452/post-201821>

You may be into this: To improve grounding, I bought a anti-static wrist strap at Radio Shack (about 10 bucks), and some copper wire at Lowes. Wrapped the wire around the copper baseboard pipe ( I checked that there was no voltage). Clipped the anti-static strap (comes with an alligator clip) to the wire, and voila!, grounded at night while sleeping. First effects so far have been pretty good sleep and very intense dreams (kinda of a good sign about energy flow). It's a way to get more free electrons. I do feel "lighter" in the morning as a result of doing this.

I've also purchased a couple of other "kits" that allow you to plug into the ground port of the outlet, and clip to your wrist. Gonna experiment with that at work since I have a desk job. I do feel a bit of a tingling with both methods. That seems to be a common experience based on other research. (I did check the outlets in home and work to be sure they are properly grounded with simple device that's also pretty cheap, and may come with some kits).

I also use the wrist strap with alligator clip in the car while driving; clip to the seat rail. I checked my seat rail for millivoltage and none was recorded.

### Tricia McVey — 2016-09-23T22:40:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tricias-journal-2.18452/post-201825>

Hi Joe!

Nice creativity! What do you mean the copper baseboard pipe ?

Tricia

### Joe Gavin — 2016-09-23T22:51:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tricias-journal-2.18452/post-201827>

i have what are known are hot water baseboard heating elements. they use use hot water for heating purposes that travels via copper pipes. not all living quarters have it; many have have forced-hot air heating (usually goes with central AC). if you don't have the HWBB, you can use the grounding kit into the outlet, or you can set up a grounding rod outside, thread the wire through a window and clip on that way. Another variation is to use those stick-on pad they use EKGs; the the kit uses a similar snap-on contraption for the wrist-strap. so you can stick a pad on your body (bottom of foot is alleged to be ideal) and get grounded.

### Valuation Man — 2016-09-20T19:58:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-street-article-good-dirty.18453/post-201661>

[http://www.wsj.com/articles/get-your-children-good-and-dirty-1473950250](http://www.wsj.com/articles/get-your-children-good-and-dirty-1473950250)

In Saturday's WSJ there was an article that encourage parents getting their kids outside and getting dirty for their health benefit. Interesting early in the article it discussed how we are living longer but certain diseases (diabetes, AI, Asthma and etc.) were increasing at a very high rate and it said some of these diseases are doubling every 10 years and occurring earlier than ever in kids.

Does not provide all the right answers but I thought it was interesting that a well-known national publication on Finance would have an article about this. At least it encourages getting your kids outside but too focused on genetics and diet as cause of rise in diseases.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-09-20T21:04:55+0300
