### prime1 — 2016-12-21T01:53:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-floater.18814/post-205567>

> [Dain Sorensen said:](/goto/post?id=204669)
>
> Hello,
>
> I am new to this forum and am wondering if anyone here suffers from eye floaters? I gained these bastards approx 3 years ago and I feel my environment is the reason. Any feedback is appreciated.
>
> Thanks
> Dain

floaters:

Retinal dopaminergic amacrine (DA) cells synthesize both dopamine and GABA. Both neurotransmitters are released over the entire cell surface and act on neighboring and distant neurons by volume transmission, but, in addition, DA cells establish GABAergic synapses onto AII amacrine cells, the neurons that transfer rod signals to cone bipolars.

DA cells exert complex functions in the retina: dopamine is released upon illumination, diffuses throughout the retina and acts by volume transmission on most types of retinal neurons, setting their gain for vision in bright light. In other words, the retinal dopaminergic circuit is a visual signal amplifier. The physiological significance of the GABA released by DA cells has not been established with certainty: by inhibiting DA cells’ postsynaptic target, GABA may prevent signals of the saturated rods from entering the cone pathway when the dark-adapted retina is suddenly exposed to bright illumination. The retinal GABAergic circuit is a visual signal attenuator, and also acts a retinal melatonin activator. Dimming the light can help you increase the retinal melatonin release for sleeping or relaxation.

If a retinal dopamine neuron can not clear GABA fast enough in response to light transition, you will see an eye floater in your visual field.
If a retinal dopamine neuron can not clear GABA all the time under light, you will see an eye floater all the time when you expose your eyes to light. The eye floater size depends on how many DA cells get involved.

easy to fix

### Dimitri — 2016-12-22T02:24:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-floater.18814/post-205633>

You are not alone Dain, I also suffer from moderate eye floaters. They are in my right eye which interestingly enough is slightly myopic, but not to the point where I need to wear glasses. My left eye which is actually slightly beyond 20/20 does not have any floaters, however I have trouble seeing up really close with that eye.

I acquired them when I was young probably around the age of 14, right around the time my myopia also developed in the right eye. The only significant lifestyle change that happened around that time was I started spending considerably more time at the computer as I received my first computer game for Christmas that year. I may have had some biologic concrete as Dr. Kruse calls it by that time since I was not yet on the paleo diet which I did not discover until I was 19, but then again my diet was not that bad in the insulin department although maybe all the grains and pasta I was eating did more glycation damage than I realize. I was already drinking non-fluoridated water for at least a couple years by that time because we thought it was bad for other reasons as we did not know about it being a dielectric blocker. I remember eating fish about twice a week as a child and I didn't spend that much time in front of screens either. We were also some of the last people to adopt wifi which I did not have in my house until I was almost 17 probably. I also spent a lot of time outside as a kid without sunglasses on as I would play outside almost every day after school until I hit middle school.

I eventually developed adrenal fatigue when I was 19, and at the time I also got mild tinnitus in my right ear but not my left. So I know that the tinnitus was not caused by loud noises or improper use of headphones. Dr. Kruse mentioned to me that my cell phone was the likely culprit of both my tinnitus and eye floaters since they are both on my right side, and I had been using a basic flip phone for a couple of years prior to receiving the floaters although I used it very conservatively and not every day.

I have only been following Dr. Kruse's protocols for about six months now and I know these things can take time, but I will keep you posted and please let us know if you make any progress.

### Dain Sorensen — 2016-12-23T07:46:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-floater.18814/post-205700>

> [prime1 said:](/goto/post?id=205567)
>
> floaters:
>
> Retinal dopaminergic amacrine (DA) cells synthesize both dopamine and GABA. Both neurotransmitters are released over the entire cell surface and act on neighboring and distant neurons by volume transmission, but, in addition, DA cells establish GABAergic synapses onto AII amacrine cells, the neurons that transfer rod signals to cone bipolars.
>
> DA cells exert complex functions in the retina: dopamine is released upon illumination, diffuses throughout the retina and acts by volume transmission on most types of retinal neurons, setting their gain for vision in bright light. In other words, the retinal dopaminergic circuit is a visual signal amplifier. The physiological significance of the GABA released by DA cells has not been established with certainty: by inhibiting DA cells’ postsynaptic target, GABA may prevent signals of the saturated rods from entering the cone pathway when the dark-adapted retina is suddenly exposed to bright illumination. The retinal GABAergic circuit is a visual signal attenuator, and also acts a retinal melatonin activator. Dimming the light can help you increase the retinal melatonin release for sleeping or relaxation.
>
> If a retinal dopamine neuron can not clear GABA fast enough in response to light transition, you will see an eye floater in your visual field.
> If a retinal dopamine neuron can not clear GABA all the time under light, you will see an eye floater all the time when you expose your eyes to light. The eye floater size depends on how many DA cells get involved.
>
>
> easy to fix

Thanks for taking the time to reply. I wish I could understand what you are telling me. If this is easy to fix. Please tell me what I need to do. These floaters fill both of my eyes and have caused stress to my everyday life. Do I need to expose myself to more natural light?

Thanks again.
Dain

### Dain Sorensen — 2016-12-23T07:56:21+0200

My left eye which is actually slightly beyond 20/20 does not have any floaters, however I have trouble seeing up really close with that eye.
>
> I acquired them when I was young probably around the age of 14, right around the time my myopia also developed in the right eye. The only significant lifestyle change that happened around that time was I started spending considerably more time at the computer as I received my first computer game for Christmas that year. I may have had some biologic concrete as Dr. Kruse calls it by that time since I was not yet on the paleo diet which I did not discover until I was 19, but then again my diet was not that bad in the insulin department although maybe all the grains and pasta I was eating did more glycation damage than I realize. I was already drinking non-fluoridated water for at least a couple years by that time because we thought it was bad for other reasons as we did not know about it being a dielectric blocker. I remember eating fish about twice a week as a child and I didn't spend that much time in front of screens either. We were also some of the last people to adopt wifi which I did not have in my house until I was almost 17 probably. I also spent a lot of time outside as a kid without sunglasses on as I would play outside almost every day after school until I hit middle school.
>
> I eventually developed adrenal fatigue when I was 19, and at the time I also got mild tinnitus in my right ear but not my left. So I know that the tinnitus was not caused by loud noises or improper use of headphones. Dr. Kruse mentioned to me that my cell phone was the likely culprit of both my tinnitus and eye floaters since they are both on my right side, and I had been using a basic flip phone for a couple of years prior to receiving the floaters although I used it very conservatively and not every day.
>
> I have only been following Dr. Kruse's protocols for about six months now and I know these things can take time, but I will keep you posted and please let us know if you make any progress.

Thanks for the reply. I will happily update. My tinnitus was also not caused by loud noise. I have had my hearing tested and it is fine. Just the less background noise, the louder it gets.

# Thread: Hands in or Hands out?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sfast — 2016-12-04T06:33:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-204746>

Hi folks,

I am progressing with my CT and fluctuating around the 20-30min at the moment. The water temperature is 32°F. So it is cold and perhaps colder than recommended but as I have just a barrel on my balcony I cannot regulate it properly. In winter, I am making a Wim Hof, meaning that I have a lot of ice.

I have a problem with my hands. After a while they start to hurt which makes my progress slow down. Are there any disadvantages for keeping (just) the hands out of the water? I am thinking of the great density of receptors in the hands and their special role as sensors of the environment.

Thanks for the help
Sascha

### Danco3636 — 2016-12-04T07:05:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-204747>

Your in freezing water 32°F for 20-30 minutes submerged? Is this in the AM or PM?

### sfast — 2016-12-04T07:41:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-204748>

Yes. Just a bit of shoulders and the head are out because I am pretty tall.

I do my CT second thing in the morning after my mobility and some movement practice. Which is around 6:30-6:45 AM

### Carole traole — 2016-12-04T10:38:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-204751>

Hi there, I am experiencing a similar problem with my hands. I am swimming every morning in an outdoor pond that is currently at 4 degrees Celsius and I am only managing to swim for 10 minutes because my fingers get really numb and painful. I see most of the other women swimming there wearing gloves and socks but I don't think I want to go down that path. I am hoping that my resilience will improve with repeated exposures but this hasn't happened just yet.

### Lahelada — 2016-12-04T13:27:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-204755>

Gloves and socks if you can swim with them are ok. Read these [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/wearing-socks.667/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/wearing-socks.667/) [https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-easy-start-guide/](https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-easy-start-guide/)

By teh way,if ever in doubt , a google search with Jack Kruse and then the term of enquiry works wonders. In this case I asked Jack Kruse socks . Not all queries have to me multisyllabic !!  I am saying this as it makes learning faster.

### Sue-UK — 2016-12-04T15:50:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-204757>

> [sfast said:](/goto/post?id=204746)
>
> Hi folks,
>
> I am progressing with my CT and fluctuating around the 20-30min at the moment. The water temperature is 32°F. So it is cold and perhaps colder than recommended but as I have just a barrel on my balcony I cannot regulate it properly. In winter, I am making a Wim Hof, meaning that I have a lot of ice.
>
> I have a problem with my hands. After a while they start to hurt which makes my progress slow down. Are there any disadvantages for keeping (just) the hands out of the water? I am thinking of the great density of receptors in the hands and their special role as sensors of the environment.
>
> Thanks for the help
> Sascha

I keep my hands out, or sometimes dip my wrists in with my fingers stretched upwards. I'm never fully submerged to the top of the neck because of my bath set up, so I will sometimes splash exposed torso/leg parts with the cold water or dip them in and out and when my hands get too cold I warm them on my carotids, or massage my scalp and face. Cools them down and my hands warm up ... Win win ...

### sfast — 2016-12-04T22:07:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-204778>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=204755)
>
> Gloves and socks if you can swim with them are ok. Read these [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/wearing-socks.667/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/wearing-socks.667/) [https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-easy-start-guide/](https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-easy-start-guide/)
>
> By teh way,if ever in doubt , a google search with Jack Kruse and then the term of enquiry works wonders. In this case I asked Jack Kruse socks . Not all queries have to me multisyllabic !!  I am saying this as it makes learning faster.

Thanks for the links. I found them. But I am not referring to the practical issue of adapt them to the cold but rather to their role as one of the main sensors during ct. The question is: Is submerging the hands more important than a faster progress without them submerged?

### Sue-UK — 2016-12-05T13:51:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-204801>

> [sfast said:](/goto/post?id=204778)
>
> Thanks for the links. I found them. But I am not referring to the practical issue of adapt them to the cold but rather to their role as one of the main sensors during ct. The question is: Is submerging the hands more important than a faster progress without them submerged?

I don't currently think so .... My feeling is that as a bipedal mammal, in a natural environment, our feet adapt differently using the gradual lowering of temperature and light stimulus as the season changes to slowly adapt, including perhaps a change in the fat ratios in our "hooves." They become less sensitive to the cold, and help endurance.

Being upright, not connected to the possibly frozen earth, there is less advantage to the hands, and more sensitivity there might have advantages, such as the collecting of food. A bipedal wading through cold water looking for food might be constantly dipping their hands in and out to harvest, but because its not chronic, they can retain sensitivity, and are still receiving information from the natural environment. Hands trailing in the water means faster heat loss and
might mean less food harvesting time ...?

### sfast — 2016-12-11T09:15:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-205123>

Thanks for your reply, Sue.

This is a good argument but I don't think it is valid. Now, we are bipedal. But we inherited many traits of our non-human ancestors. The spine is developed in the 3D-World of the water and still benefits from water like actions (e.g. spinal waves) and we can find residues of quadruple locomotion in our brain, which can explain, why quadruple locomotion is oddly beneficial in ones movement practice.

I apply this thinking to the hands. Yes, we use them in a different way, but the question remains: Could it be oddly beneficial to adapt the hands while submerged in water? When we talk about cold adaption we no longer talk about being exposed to a natural environment. We talk about ice baths which is a artificial way to have a connection to nature again.

### Sue-UK — 2016-12-11T18:36:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-205161>

> [sfast said:](/goto/post?id=205123)
>
> Thanks for your reply, Sue.
>
> This is a good argument but I don't think it is valid. Now, we are bipedal. But we inherited many traits of our non-human ancestors. The spine is developed in the 3D-World of the water and still benefits from water like actions (e.g. spinal waves) and we can find residues of quadruple locomotion in our brain, which can explain, why quadruple locomotion is oddly beneficial in ones movement practice.
>
> I apply this thinking to the hands. Yes, we use them in a different way, but the question remains: Could it be oddly beneficial to adapt the hands while submerged in water? When we talk about cold adaption we no longer talk about being exposed to a natural environment. We talk about ice baths which is a artificial way to have a connection to nature again.

Quantum Biology 6 - Bipedalism has made me think differently about the hands compared to the feet.

For me there is a "temperature window of opportunity" for cold adaptation for the hands, and that when the temperature drops to a certain level, and dependent on other environmental factors, the risks can outweigh the potential benefits. But its a constantly changing N=1. Shrinking my respiratory proteins and reducing inflammation systemically is more important to me. I aim to be outside in the water just before sunrise. I can tolerate 0C water with a clear sky and good view of the rising sun much better than 12C water under wall to wall cloud, the light makes a big difference, so I can't say I can tolerate xC for x minutes with hands submerged or not submerged, because it varies dependent on the environment as a whole. As a general rule I am much more observant of skin changes when the water temp goes below 5C, and more so if there is also a very cold wind. The only time I don't CT outside is in very strong winds which grounds even the larger birds, or during thunderstorms.

On my second session this morning, just dipping my hands in and out of the water, I reached a point where I could see that from the knuckles to the fingertips were going white .... time to get out, before it gets painful (which could raise cortisol). Get out whilst I'm a woman in the cold not a cold woman.  Had I kept them submerged, I would have been out much faster, and had I done it during the first session, I may not have got in for the second session 30 minutes later. So by not keeping my hands and feet submerged, I gained extra time in the water, keeping my spine submerged, and it was challenging without being stressful/painful. I think the spine is a better systemic environmental temperature sensor than the hands, particularly for women. In an icy wind, women will normally turn their backs to it, protecting their breasts. I've read that ice cold breasts can stop breast milk ... so in evolutionary terms this behaviour sort of makes sense, and because cold on the back stimulates BAT to generate heat, even more important because women generally have less muscle.

### sfast — 2016-12-13T09:59:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-205206>

I think different context is important: I am a fit and healthy guy who doesn't want to fix broken health but to get after it. I don't mind the pain (up to a point of course). I am more on the antifragile road and not so into fixing fragilities.

However, one could test the hypothesis we would need one group that cold adapts with hands submerged and one with the hands out. Afterwards, they have to be tested with hands out for comparison. I do both. I have two 24h-fasting days (only eating supper) and I do only approximately half of the duration I do on my normal days but with hands in. After I adopted this practice, my cold tolerance seemed to expand a bit faster.

Of course, with hands out I am able to be in the cold for quite a bit longer. But this does not mean that the adaptions are better. Like in endurance training: Some adaptions are better when interval training even though you are reducing the overall training volume.

### drezy — 2017-04-07T01:30:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-in-or-hands-out.18816/post-211941>

> [sfast said:](/goto/post?id=205206)
>
> However, one could test the hypothesis we would need one group that cold adapts with hands submerged and one with the hands out. Afterwards, they have to be tested with hands out for comparison.

Good points but I fear that groups of people, all with different exposures nnEMF and degenerative issues, might still not match up with your own personal N=1.

I'm in a similar boat as you as far as not having any obvious issues at this point, doing 24 hour fasting, and being a collector of Taleb books (presuming that you anti fragile reference comes from him).

When you say "with hands out I am able to be in the cold for quite a bit longer" roughly how much time are you talking? I'll try out both alternatives and see what I notice. So far I've done hands in and out and usually just stay in for a 48 min timer.

On a side note I once tried to rush dinner too soon after a hand-in CT session before my muscles loosened back up. I ended up slicing right into 1/10 of my left hand index finger knuckle. It should have gushed like wild, but instead I got a good look at what a dorsal digital artery was. The cut itself healed really quick too FWIW.

# Thread: Immune system
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/>
**Subforum:** Heal Your Hormones
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mads Haerup — 2016-12-04T14:03:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204756>

Hi, I have the recent years had Fungus on my feet and in the groin, congested nose and sore throat multiple times, hives, a coughing period lasting for several months, and mucus stuck in my throat which doesn't seem to go away. I seem to be very susceptible to getting a cold and sore throat. So I was wondering, something must be wrong with my immune system, since this happens. I have visited a few doctors, but they just prescribe medicine or tell you it will go away. I'm not a fan of that approach though. So I'm seeking some answers of what I should look for if I choose to get an immune system test done, and which hormones might play a role in this madness.
My hair has also started to thinning out, it started when I turned 18, it's in my genetics as my father is bald and my big brother has almost lost all his. If any has any inputs on how to delay or treat it, then speak your mind. (I have read jack Kruse article on this topic.)
A little about myself: 21 years, male, living in Denmark and therefore won't get exposed to as much sun which would be optimal, I'm considering investing in a humancharger to solve this problem. I rarely consume alcoholic drinks. I only eat organic food, mostly plant based due to the high prices of organic meat, I eat meat 1-3 times a week though. I don't consume junk food, sugary food/beverages, eating as little gluten as possible. I often fast for 12-14 hours, So I skip breakfast every morning. I have an active job as a demolisher, I'm walking around 10 km/6 miles a day, and then I do strength based workout 3-4 times a week. I workout on an empty stomach 2 times a week. I have no allergies, dairy tolerant, gluten tolerant. I drink tons of herbal tea a day, consume broccoli, spinach, sweet potato, blueberries, avocado almost every day. So I should get an adequate amount of antioxidants. Supplements I take: Organic Spirulina powder, Ginger capsules, MSM, Green lipped mussels, Fish oil either Artic COD liver oil or an organic danish fish oil. I try to limit my exposure to harmful chemicals, I only use organic personal hygiene products and organic/sustainable soap for dishes, clothes, and for cleaning my home.

Last info: I Broke my foot 4 months ago and have been exposed to CT-scan 2 times this year, and as I understand CT emits a fairly amount of radiation which also could play a role in altering my cells and immune system.
Hope some of you can help me out a bit
Cheers!

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-04T20:27:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204767>

When you are missing Selenium semiconductors in your tissues diseases like mold/candida/fungus/cancer are much more likely because of a loss of immunoregulation. Selenium is needed for the proper functioning of the immune system cells because of how it moves electrons and works with sunlight. It is a co-factor in the activation of neutrophils, macrophages, and Natural killer cells and T lymphocytes and many other immunoregulatory functions as laid out in Ferencik’s paper in 2003. Selenium seems to act via solid state mechanisms by two modes to protect us from cancer. One is that is catalyzes a many immune reactions to increase our immune function in the GALT. Secondly, Se seems to hinder oncogenesis by protecting the p53 gene by adding electrons to this gene in the gut mitochondria and does not allow oncogenesis to occur. In fact, it appears to favor low level inflammation by altering NRF2 pathways. This is seen in infectious diseases and is seen in autoimmune conditions vs. cancer, and thus, favors apoptosis of defective cells instead of oncogenesis.

It appears that low plasma Selenium levels reduce the production of plasma glutathione peroxidase. Patients with low selenium levels are known to have higher rates of polyps and autoimmunity. (Fernandez-Banares et al., 2002) Naturally occurring Selenium is found in plants like garlic and broccoli as a compound called Se-methylselenocysteine (Se-MSC). Seafood is the best source!!! Se-MSc produces 33% reduction in cancerous lesions (colon) than selenite (synthetic). It turns out cysteine is really important too with Selenium. It also produces a 50% decrease in new tumor development locally in the gut. It also induces cells to undergo apoptosis and not epi-oncogenesis. It has been shown to reduce angiogenesis to make the cellular terroir less hospitable to cancer. Se- MSC also directly down regulates vascular endothelial growth factor that is commonly found in most GI cancers. So the story seems to show that selenium is pretty critical to gut health and disease prevention because it is an N-type semiconductor.

In fact, many people believe that cystic fibrosis is a severe form of selenium deficiency in the germ cell line and not a real genetic disease. This is controversial today, but I believe it has legs. It has also been seen in numerous papers that in both autoimmune and oncogenesis disease that sulfated vitamin D3 and Selenium decreases are seen much more commonly linked. Vitamin K2 production by the gut bacteria is also down regulated in these cases as well in inflammatory conditions like cancer, autoimmunity and inflammatory bowel diseases. It appears that chronic gut inflammation allows for the reduction of both selenium, sulfated vitamin D3 and vitamin K2. All of these molcules are photoactive with sunlight because of their carbon cages in the aromatic amino acids and quinone rings. These rings act like a play ground for photons to build up the DC electric current in cells used to activate the immune system. Vitamin K2 reduction also shows a very strong correlation with many neolithic diseases as well tied to poor solar exposure.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-04T20:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204768>

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27217104](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27217104)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-04T20:30:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204770>

Every single study you find links dietary marine intake correlating with decreases in inflammation. Why?
These foods have things like B12 and Se in them, therefore they collects photons and move electrons in these tissues is the short answer. Where ever you problem is with inflammation realize you need DHA and the co-factors buried in the marine seafood chain.

Consider the cell mediated immune system. Its most critical cell that activates both arms of immunity need the macrophage to work.

We've known for a long time that DHA tames inflammation, now, we learn exactly how DHA works: via new substances called maresins. These chemicals are breakdown chaperone proteins from DHA that change Macrophages from inflammatory to anti inflammatory actors in the immune system. This means that macrophages change their function with the addition or subtraction of electrons in maresins.
DHA does this by switching them from M1 to M2 phenotypes in this immune cell line. This cell is very commonly found in the human GI tract. If you got a gut problem........you need the Epi paleo Rx.

You need oysters. If you do not like them........then enjoy your illness.

This is a critical spot in autoimmune activation. We encounter inflammation almost daily in our species, but our body has ways of turning it off naturally. DHA is that switch in CMI in macrophages. This is an important step toward understanding exactly this happens. You're likely to be hearing a lot more about maresins when medicine realizes that DHA is an electron story.

In the July 2013 issue of The FASEB Journal, researchers found that macrophages use DHA to produce the chaperone like "maresins," which serve as the "switch" that turns inflammation off and switches on resolution in cell mediated reactions.

CITES:
Jesmond Dalli, Min Zhu, Nikita A. Vlasenko, Bin Deng, Jesper Z. Haeggström, Nicos A. Petasis, and Charles N. Serhan. The novel 13S,14S-epoxy-maresin is converted by human macrophages to maresin 1 (MaR1), inhibits leukotriene A4 hydrolase (LTA4H), and shifts macrophage phenotype. FASEB J July 2013 27:2573-2583; doi:10.1096/fj.13-227728

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-04T20:33:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204772>

Selenium deficiency is associated with many cancer lines as well. Selenium deficiency has been shown to cause global hypomethylation in DNA. This links it to B-12, another solar vitamin. This is where the link of infectious disease, autoimmunity and oncogenesis seems to lie. Hypo-methylation is the “on and off switch” between the these two disease processes. Hypo-methylation is more apparent when DNA is not well hydrated in vivo. The co factors important for switch activation are selenium, sulfated Vitamin D3, and bacterial Vitamin K2 production in the leaky gut. The differing concentrations of all three seem to predispose to what cellular fate or field will be faced in presence of a leaky gut. The less aggressive cellular terroir may predispose us GI distress with bloating, malnutrition and bad bowel habits. It may progress to an elevated sdLDL cholesterol that causes chronic neolithic diseases like heart disease, Crohn’s disease or diabetes. More significant inflammation likely elevates IL-1b and favors autoimmune conditions to blossom. This is associated with NF kappa beta and IL-6 spikes in the brain by way of the vagus nerve. This is when the blood brain barrier becomes leaky. We can assess this with a "GABA test" clinically. Further inflammation changes the mix of the co factors and the cytokines to favor frank oncogenesis in many of our gut organs. We have no idea what is critical to each step but we do know the players today. So if you alter your solar exposure and diet toward seafood make sure each one is plentiful you can control the master switch in your own “leaky gut” to control your outcome. When it comes to the leaky blood brain barrier, the key issue seems to be an environmental triggers of nnEMF and blue light that alters T helper cell maturation in the neuro-immune system. N-type semiconductors have a larger electron concentration than hole concentration. The phrase ‘n-type’ comes from the negative charge of the electron. In n-type semiconductors, electrons are the majority carriers and holes are the minority carriers. N-type semiconductors are created by doping an intrinsic semiconductor with donor impurities (or doping a p-type semiconductor as done in the making of CMOS chips). A common dopant for n-type silicon in technology is phosphorus. Life does the same thing, but it pairs carbon with nitrogen and phosphorus in its semiconductive circuits. Selenium works with Ca2+ and Mg2+. Physics geeks will recall in an N-type semiconductor, the Fermi level is greater than that of the intrinsic semiconductor and lies closer to the conduction band than the valence band. You might want to read the Time 21 blog.

# Thread: The place you choose to get an MRI and those who read it have implications
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-place-you-choose-to-get-an-mri-and-those-who-read-it-have-implications.18819/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-05T04:14:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-place-you-choose-to-get-an-mri-and-those-who-read-it-have-implications.18819/post-204787>

If you have not listened to the podcast Idid with Ben Greenfield on his MRI and what I found on it you might find it interesting. Have a listen to it over at his site. Most radiologists are not trained to see images of the redox potential so what you get is very uneven results. The redox potential of the people reading your scan often matters more than the redox potential in the scan!!!! Most people never get to chose where to have their scan done so they have no idea what variables matter. Moreover, they never meet the radiologist to get a sense of his or her own redox potential which is a critical vriable too. Most radiologists spend their shift in a dark room surrounded by blue light monitors for 8-10 hours a days. Usually experience grows as one ages in medical fields. I think in radiology we might have question that axiom because of what they are doing to their eye clock. In the last 15 years I have not met one radiologist who is not mypoic of who does not wear glasses of contacts yet no one ever thinks about what this means to the frontal lobes attached to those eyes. In radiology, this might mean that the longer they do this job, the worse they maybe at interpreting scans. This is why I always look at who gave me a reading of a scan and then I really dissect the report with the image myself. This lets me observe if the reading is coherent to the image or not. This is a trick I learned long ago from one of my mentors. It is a bigger deal as time has elapsed because of how neuroimaging has changed. Now we have this study belwo that shows you this phenomena has some real clinical consequences that rarely get spoken about. You can find the podcast I did with Ben here. [https://bengreenfieldfitness.com/2016/11/measuring-redox-potential-with-mri/](https://bengreenfieldfitness.com/2016/11/measuring-redox-potential-with-mri/)

MRI's are basically quantum super position images of proton shadows. They are subject to machine/image bias and observer bias because both machine and eye reading it are quantum machines that work on probability and uncertainty principles. The real issue.........no one realizes it. Well, some of us do and I hope you consider the implications of this post.

STUDY: Patient gets 10 MRIs done at different facilities on her lumbar spine in a 3 week period and the reports are all over the place.

Where you go and who reads the scans can be the difference between you getting the right diagnosis and the wrong one. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/27867079/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/27867079/)

Is having an MRI bad for me, having HD? I used to participate in the HOPES study for HD and underwent an MRI every two years for about 6 years. I always came out of the machine with massive brain fog.

When I had surgery to "repair" the injury, what was found was not what was "seen" on the MRI. Since then I have been a bit skeptical of MRIs.

### JoeT — 2016-12-06T04:19:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nj.18824/post-204846>

Hi All, new member here, first posting!

This seems like a great community, and I am looking forward to participating. I'd like to thank all of you, especially Mr. Kruse, for all of the knowledge sharing!

A little about me: I luckily don't have any pressing health issues...I'm always trying to improve the way I feel, and my performance. I'm not even sure how I stumbled on to Jack's work; it may have been either the Greenfield or Ausprey podcasts.

Unfortunately, I don't really have a passion for reading Science; I wish I did, but I don't. That being said, I find Jack's work reading the superior importance of light, grounding, and water to be absolutely brilliant.
So simple, yet so overlooked. Everything we need is right here in front of us, if we can just ditch our modern ways a little bit. So, while I have only skimmed many of the blogs and Q&A's, I am excited to see how my body reacts to application of the concepts.

Due to family comitments, I can't leave NJ, so a lot of my work thus far has been in measuring, eliminating, and reducing EMFs, and increasing Light. In my first two weeks, I've implemented the following, and am planning a lot more:

1\. 10-15 minutes outside at sunrise; no glasses and grounded.
2\. purchased a trifield and made numerous little adjustments at work and at home, including moving the room I sleep in, and position of computers.
3\. Uvex after dark.
4\. More sun at 11 am and 1 pm
5\. Added sardines and shrimp weekly
6\. Ordered Thera Blue glasses for Office
7\. Phone is now almost always on airplane
8\. Shut off Wifi at night.
9\. I've always drank at least 72 oz of RO per day, so no change there.

Things I'm working On
1\. Getting Baseline lab work....my last Bun to Creatine was 13. My cholesterol has always been sensitive to Saturated Fat.
2\. Getting a better meter for RF; getting Ethernet back.
3\. Potentially starting to drive to work instead of taking train....pending.
4\. Formally following one or two JK protocols, probably CT.
5\. Just got Jack's book.
6\. If i start to see real results from the above, I will almost certainly buy a magnetico.

I'm new to Epi-Paleo, and am trying to adjust my schedule, routine, and habits to get as much sun as possible given my office job. I'm up to 15 minutes at 730 am, and 10 minutes at 11 am., and 10 minutes at 1 pm. Just wondering, based on the below, if I should add some lighting in my office to offset the standard florescent? Also, if I picked up the UVB Desert light, I'm guessing I would want to start with just a minute or two every few days, correct?
\==============================================
UVB Desert light
is non descriptive
but I am guessing that you mean:
**Exo Terra Solar-Glo High Intensity Self-Ballasted Uv/Heat Mercury Vapor Lamp, 160-Watt by Exo Terra**
[https://www.amazon.com/Exo-Terra-So...qid=1481202048&sr=8-7&keywords=Exo+Terra+160w](https://www.amazon.com/Exo-Terra-Solar-Glo-Intensity-Self-Ballasted/dp/B01M4NCYAY/ref=sr_1_7?ie=UTF8&qid=1481202048&sr=8-7&keywords=Exo+Terra+160w)

This are good full spectrum lamps.
They are meant to provide full daylight needs for a lizard.
Note, lizard will be real close to such a lamp.
Light power decreases wit a square root of distance.
So, if lizard is ok having one lamp at the distance of 2 feet
At the distance of 4 feet he will need four of such a lamps to get same amount of light.
Practically, have maximum practical amount of this lamps in place where you spend most of the time,
and be aware that you will still be missing on light
if your ideal light
is light in tropics.

Speaking of light, we need full spectrum,
starting with UVb, UVa, ... blue, ... red, IR-A, heat
@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) discussions, and references (dr Wunch)
high some specific frequencies
UVb--vitamin B
UVa--vitamin A
blue (460-480nm), pineal gland, melatonin/cortisol/Circadian rhythm, on at wakeup, off at least 2-4hrs before bed time
Red, health, natural night vision
IR-A, health, night vision with instruments
heat, free energy that we live off
\------

In Woodbridge NJ you will always be short of natural UVa & UVb
I got for my self Red Sperti and use it 15min/day, average 5-6 days/week
[https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001ID8P78/ref=sr\_ph\_1\_a\_it?ie=UTF8&qid=1481203063&sr=sr-1&keywords=sperti](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001ID8P78/ref=sr_ph_1_a_it?ie=UTF8&qid=1481203063&sr=sr-1&keywords=sperti)

Question: I have managed to get my Office down to .5 to .8 milligause, and my RF also is reading very low. This is surprising I think since I'm basically sitting on the NJ side of the Hudson, staring out at the WTC and Empire State buildings!

So, one way I got the MG down was to shut off a florescent light that hovers about 2 feet over my desk (not a ceiling light). That was putting out around 2 Mg at 2 feet away.

So I'm wondering, would adding a light like the one below to get some increased UV (in addition to wearing my blue block glasses), have benefits that would outweigh the increased Mg that it would likely expose me to ? I recognize natural light is always best, its just very very difficult for me to get out there during peak times.

[https://www.amazon.com/Zoo-Med-ReptiSun®-Fluorescent-48-Inch/dp/B001F95Z1Y](https://www.amazon.com/Zoo-Med-ReptiSun%C2%AE-Fluorescent-48-Inch/dp/B001F95Z1Y)

### JanSz — 2016-12-09T22:24:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nj.18824/post-205065>

> [JoeT said:](/goto/post?id=205062)
>
> Question: I have managed to get my Office down to .5 to .8 milligause, and my RF also is reading very low. This is surprising I think since I'm basically sitting on the NJ side of the Hudson, staring out at the WTC and Empire State buildings!
>
> So, one way **I got the MG down was to shut off a florescent light that hovers about 2 feet over my desk** (not a ceiling light). That was putting out around 2 Mg at 2 feet away.
>
> So I'm wondering, would adding a light like the one below **to get some increased UV (in addition to wearing my blue block glasses),** have benefits that would outweigh the increased Mg that it would likely expose me to ? I recognize natural light is always best, its just very very difficult for me to get out there during peak times.
>
> [https://www.amazon.com/Zoo-Med-ReptiSun®-Fluorescent-48-Inch/dp/B001F95Z1Y](https://www.amazon.com/Zoo-Med-ReptiSun%C2%AE-Fluorescent-48-Inch/dp/B001F95Z1Y)

We are talking office.
I assume that you are in that office at the time when outside is a daylight.
Natural daylight contains blue light.
I think that one do not need blue-blockers during daytime (anytime).
If I follow Jack's recommendations correctly,
there is one lady here, that have eye disease and works in windowless building with fluorescent lights only.
She was a candidate for blue-blockers.
Possibly few others.
Blue-blockers have a logical application after sundown, to protect (mostly) pineal gland or other areas that may be sensitive to blue.
\--
About UV frequencies.
We need them to produce vit A (UVa) and vit D (UVb)
with help of
carotenoids (carrots, peppers)
and
sulfur (garlic, onions, MSM)
Using UV lamps during the day may be nice conversation piece.
But, you may also discover some proteins left by wandering rodents.
Those lights are also called actinic.
As far as I can tell, UV light does not affect our Circadian Rhythm.
We can have exposure to UV any time.
I feel more secure of getting sufficient UV exposure by
not taking vit D supplements
I still take vit A (but not sure)
use most sunlight within plus minus 2 hrs of solar noon
use Red Sperti. I use 15min/day about 5-6x/week

Trust but verify.
I use Spectracell Micronutient Analysis to figure (functional) level of 35 of them, including A&D
\-------------------------------

Solar noon.
In summer, in NJ, we have summer daylight saving time, clock is pushed 1hr ahead.
in summer the window for best available UV light is 11AM-3PM
in winter we are back to 10AM-2PM
\------------------------------

### JoeT — 2016-12-17T17:39:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nj.18824/post-205398>

Jan, I've been reading, researching, and listening to Q&A podcasts to try to enhance my light conditions, and have come up with the following plan, based on what is feasible given my current schedule. Can you tell me if it appears reasonable and balanced?

\- Firstly, given my office job, I'm exposed to a lot of nnEMF blue light. I am maximizing every available minute to get natural light. I watch the sun rise for 15 minutes each morning, and go outside for lunch for one hour at noon. So, i unfortunately I think I need some artificial light.

\-I don't think the Solar Glo's will work for me because 1) I'm not even sure my employer would allow it, and 2) I am still exposed to significant artificial blue light at work, so if I understand Jack correctly, I think it would be more beneficial to block all of the nnEMF blue light at work. This will require me to wear BlueTechs, which is fine.

\-During a podcast, Jack mentioned that if he doesn't get adaqate sun during the day, he will "catch up" from 4-7 with the following:
black CFL 26W for UVA, along with a Ruby Lux for NIR. Do you think I should use more than one black CFL against the Ruby lux?

\- For UVB, I have two options. I can buy a Sperti; or, it so happens that a family member, due to a skin condition, has a "Panosol II" machine in the house. I'm hesitant to try it though, as it is quite powerful. Don't even know where to start with that one.

I know this is not nearly optimal, but it's what I have to work with right now.

About UV light (UVa & UVb)
we look at it at least two ways.

High intensity, producing vit A & D, less exposure time may be need, adequacy measured by blood testing.

Low intensity, requires many hours daily, measured by overall health, non-specific
See restaurant crew in Chicago's restaurant.
Also current Scompy's posting:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/scompys-journal.16641/page-37#post-205360](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/scompys-journal.16641/page-37#post-205360)
and Jack's comments about it.

UV light presence or absence does not influence Circadian Rhythm.
Dr @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) uses one black light in his daughter bedroom.
But there is a blog written by his guest, and he mentions about 40 of those in Jack's residence.
I try to replicate that situation with Exo Terra Solar Glow 160W being on about +12hrs/day.
\----------------------------------
Blue light
is observed by pineal gland to know about time of the day.
So wearing blu-blockers when (outside) sun is up is usually wrong move.
\----------------------------------
Sunlight have a range of frequencies.
Visible is its mid-range
On each side we have frequencies that are invisible to our eyes.
We need them all, we evolved using all of them.
UV as written above.
On the other side there are variety of IR frequencies then heat.

Lots of discussions on EZ water assumes availability of IR.
That is free energy that life uses to continue.
